title: Compaction 101
subtitle: Foundation Skills for Civil Construction
description: Master the fundamentals of soil and aggregate compaction for civil construction. Covering equipment operation, testing methods, and compliance standards.

lessons.title: What is Compaction?
lessons.content:
Compaction is the process of mechanically increasing the density of soil by reducing air voids. Proper compaction is critical for pavement longevity, structural integrity, and preventing settlement.

**Key Principles:**
- Reducing air voids in soil matrix
- Increasing dry density
- Improving load-bearing capacity
- Reducing permeability and settlement

**Why It Matters:**
Poor compaction is the #1 cause of premature pavement failure in New Zealand civil construction. A 1% difference in compaction can reduce pavement life by up to 30%.

lessons.title: Soil Types & Behaviour
lessons.content:
Different soils behave differently under compaction. Understanding your material is essential before choosing equipment or methods.

**Cohesive Soils (Clay):**
- Require kneading/sheepsfoot roller
- Sensitive to moisture content
- Optimal moisture is critical

**Granular Soils (Sand/Gravel):**
- Respond to vibration
- Less moisture sensitive
- Vibratory rollers most effective

**Mixed Soils:**
- Combination approach required
- Test strips recommended

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of compaction?
options: Reduce air voids and increase density

questions.q: Which roller type is best for cohesive/clay soils?

lessons.title: Roller Types & Selection
lessons.content:
**Smooth Drum Vibratory Rollers:**
Most common in NZ civil construction. Ideal for granular base course and asphalt. Frequency: 25-35 Hz. Amplitude: low for thin layers, high for thick.

**Padfoot/Sheepsfoot Rollers:**
For cohesive soils and subgrade preparation. Kneading action densifies clay from bottom up. Required for embankment construction.

**Pneumatic Tyre Rollers:**
For sealing and finishing. Creates kneading action across full width. Good for mixed materials.

**Plate Compactors:**
Small areas, trenches, around structures. Forward and reversible types available.

lessons.title: Pre-Start Checks & Operation
lessons.content:
**Pre-Start Checklist:**
□ Fluid levels (oil, water, hydraulic)
□ Tyre pressures (pneumatic rollers)
□ Drum condition — no flat spots or damage
□ Water spray system operational
□ ROPS/FOPS certification current
□ Daily pre-start form completed

**Operating Principles:**
- Overlap passes by 150-300mm
- Consistent speed — 3-5 km/h
- Avoid sharp turns on fresh material
- Work from edges to centre on roads
- Count and record passes

lessons.title: NZ Compaction Standards
lessons.content:
**NZTA & Local Authority Requirements:**
All compaction must meet NZ Transport Agency specifications. Key documents:
- TNZ B/2 — Pavement Layers
- TNZ F/1 — Field Density Testing
- NZS 4402 — Soil Testing Methods

**Minimum Compaction Requirements:**
- Subgrade: 95% Standard Proctor (NZTA)
- Subbase: 98% Modified Proctor
- Basecourse: 98% Modified Proctor
- Asphalt: 97% Marshall Density

**Moisture Content Windows:**
Material must be within ±2% of Optimum Moisture Content (OMC) for effective compaction.

lessons.title: Field Testing Methods
lessons.content:
**Nuclear Density Gauge (NDG):**
Most common NZ field test. Measures density and moisture in situ. Requires calibration and radiation safety training.

**Sand Replacement (Sand Cone):**
Traditional method. Highly accurate but time-consuming. Required for calibration checks.

**Clegg Impact Hammer:**
Quick field indicator. Not a substitute for density testing but useful for pattern testing.

**Scala Penetrometer:**
Measures subgrade strength (CBR correlation). Required for pavement design verification.

**Testing Frequency:**
- Minimum 1 test per 500m² per layer
- More frequent in suspect areas
- All failures require investigation and re-test

questions.q: Moisture content must be within ___% of OMC:

lessons.title: Common Failures & Causes
lessons.content:
**Rutting/Deformation:**
Causes: Insufficient compaction, wet subgrade, overloading
Solution: Remove and rework, improve drainage, reduce layer thickness

**Cracking (Transverse):**
Causes: Over-compaction of asphalt, temperature differential
Solution: Adjust roller passes, monitor mat temperature

**Shoving/Rippling:**
Causes: Too many roller passes, unstable base, overloading
Solution: Stop rolling, allow to cool/set, check base stability

**Tender Mix (Asphalt):**
Causes: High binder content, high temperature, over-vibration
Solution: Reduce vibration, wait for mat to cool to 80°C

lessons.title: Quality Assurance Records
lessons.content:
**Required Documentation:**
□ Pre-start inspection forms (daily)
□ Material delivery dockets
□ Layer thickness records
□ Roller pass counts and patterns
□ Field density test results
□ Moisture content records
□ Non-conformance reports
□ ITP (Inspection & Test Plan) sign-offs

**Digital Records:**
Photograph all test locations with GPS coordinates. Many NZ councils now require digital as-built records. Cloud backup all quality records immediately.

questions.q: Shoving/rippling in asphalt is caused by:
options: Too many passes on unstable base

questions.q: Which document outlines your quality checkpoints?

lessons.title: Course Review
lessons.content:
**Congratulations on completing Compaction 101!**

You have covered:
✅ Compaction principles and soil types
✅ Equipment selection and operation
✅ NZ compliance standards (NZTA)
✅ Field testing methods
✅ Quality assurance and documentation
